coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots as antimicrobial agents against gram-positive Bacillus subtilis and Staphylococcus aureus under visible white light-emitting diode
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zheng, Ya-Yun
Huang, Kuang-Tzu
Lee, Sin-Jen
Ni, Jen-Shyang
Hsueh, Yi-Huang
coredata.dc:description: Carbon nanomaterials, such as carbon quantum dots (CQDs), are excellent candidates for antibacterial agent development as they are cost-effective. Nitrogen-doped CQDs (N-doped CQDs) exhibit antibacterial activity against gram-positive bacteria. However, investigation of N-CQDs and their efficacy against the selected bacterial strains under visible light irradiation has not been carried out. Here, we aimed to evaluate N-CQDs for their efficacy as a photosensitizer for antimicrobial photodynamic therapy (aPDT). N-CQDs were synthesized using the hydrothermal method with 1,3,6-trinitropyrene (TNP) as the nitrogen and carbon source. Antimicrobial tests of N-CQDs were conducted against gram-positive (Bacillus subtilis NCIB 3610, Staphylococcus aureus Newman, and S. aureus USA300) and gram-negative (Escherichia coli K12) bacteria at different concentrations (0–100 mg/L). The N-CQDs exerted a moderate growth-inhibitory effect on gram-positive bacteria in the dark. However, in the presence of white light generated using 20-W light-emitting diode, N-CQDs could completely kill the bacteria at a low concentration of 20 mg/L. The minimal inhibitory concentration of N-CQDs for the two S. aureus strains (Newman and USA300) was 20 mg/L under light conditions, whereas that for B. subtilis was 100 mg/L. The results demonstrate that N-CQDs may be used as a photosensitizer in aPDT and as an antibacterial agent.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-doped carbon quantum dots
Photosensitizer
Antimicrobial photodynamic therapy
Hydrothermal synthesis
Reactive oxygen species
Antibacterial activity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Growth conditions for various bacterial strains
Flow cytometry analysis following antibacterial activity testing
Mechanism of action of N-CQDs on S. aureus activity

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was funded by the National Science and Technology Council of Taiwan to Yi-Huang Hsueh, grant numbers 112-2221-E992-012 and 113-2221-E-992 -009 -MY2 and by Chang Gung Medical Foundation of Taiwan to Kuang-Tzu Huang, grant number CMRPG8N0441.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Representation of the synthesized N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs). (a) Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image at a low magnification (100 K). (b) High-resolution (500 K) TEM (HR-TEM) image of a single N-CQD showing the lattice spacing. (c) Particle size distribution of the synthesized N-CQDs. (d) Energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectrum showing the chemical elemental composition of N-CQDs.

Characterization of the synthesized N-CQDs. (a) Raman spectra of N-CQDs. (b) UV-Vis spectra of N-CQDs. (c) Fluorescence spectrum when excited between 300 and 450 nm. (d) N-CQDs showing different potentials at the interface at different pH levels.
XPS spectra of N-CQDs. (a) Full scan X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) survey spectrum, (b) C 1 s spectrum, (c) N 1 s spectrum, and (d) O 1 s spectrum.

Reactive oxygen species (ROS) assay. FL spectra of 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein (DCFH) (1 μM) at varying irradiation periods and concentrations of N-CQDs: (a) 0, (b) 10, and (c) 20 mg/L. Excitation wavelength: 480 nm. (d) ROS curves of DCFH (1 μM) at different concentrations of N-CQDs and irradiation periods.

Antimicrobial test using different concentrations of N-CQDs against gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria under white light and dark conditions. (a) Escherichia coli K12, (b) Bacillus subtilis NCIB 3610, (c) Staphylococcus aureus Newman, (d) Staphylococcus aureus USA300. Data shown are mean ± standard deviation of two independent experiments repeated four times. (Black: cultured in the dark; White: cultured under white light).

Scanning electron microscopy images of gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria under light and dark conditions with different concentrations of N-CQDs. (a, b) Control for B. subtilis without N-CQDs. (c, d) Bacillus subtilis under light with N-CQDs (20 mg/L) (e, f) B. subtilis under dark with N-CQDs (20 mg/L). (g, h) Control for E. coli without N-CQDs. (i, j) E. coli under light with N-CQDs 20 mg/L.

Fluorescence intensity analysis of S. aureus Newman after incubation with 20 mg/L N-CQDs and staining with RedoxSensor™ Green reagent under light and dark conditions. The X-axis represents the intensity of RedoxSensor™ Green reagent in arbitrary units (a.u.) and the Y-axis represents the cell count. (a) ddH2O. (b) RedoxSensor™ Green reagent only. (c) 20 mg/L N-CQDs. (d) 20 mg/L N-CQDs + RedoxSensor™ Green reagent. (e) Staphylococcus aureus Newman cultured for 1 h. (f) Staphylococcus aureus Newman cultured for 1 h with RedoxSensor™ Green reagent. (g) Staphylococcus aureus Newman incubated with 20 mg/L N-CQDs for 1 h + RedoxSensor™ Green reagent in the dark. (h) Staphylococcus aureus Newman incubated with 20 mg/L N-CQDs for 1 h + RedoxSensor™ Green reagent under illuminated conditions.

Assessment of cytotoxicity of N-CQDs. 3T3-L1 cells were incubated in the presence of various concentrations of N-CQDs. The control groups had the same volume of solvent for each concentration. Cell growth was evaluated using the CCK-8 assay. Data are presented as mean ± standard deviation. Data from the group with no N-CQDs or solvent are set to 1.0.
Percentages of different bonding groups in the N-CQDs as determined using XPS analysis.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots as antimicrobial agents against gram-positive Bacillus subtilis and Staphylococcus aureus under visible white light-emitting diode

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical and Materials Engineering, Photo-sensitive Material Advanced Research and Technology Center (Photo-SMART), National Kaohsiung University of Science and Technology, Kaohsiung City 80778, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Materials Engineering, Photo-sensitive Material Advanced Research and Technology Center (Photo-SMART), National Kaohsiung University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical and Materials Engineering, Photo-sensitive Material Advanced Research and Technology Center (Photo-SMART), National Kaohsiung University of Science and Technology, Kaohsiung City 80778, Taiwan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon nanomaterials, such as carbon quantum dots (CQDs), are excellent candidates for antibacterial agent development as they are cost-effective. Nitrogen-doped CQDs (N-doped CQDs) exhibit antibacterial activity against gram-positive bacteria. However, investigation of N-CQDs and their efficacy against the selected bacterial strains under visible light irradiation has not been carried out. Here, we aimed to evaluate N-CQDs for their efficacy as a photosensitizer for antimicrobial photodynamic therapy (aPDT). N-CQDs were synthesized using the hydrothermal method with 1,3,6-trinitropyrene (TNP) as the nitrogen and carbon source. Antimicrobial tests of N-CQDs were conducted against gram-positive (Bacillus subtilis NCIB 3610, Staphylococcus aureus Newman, and S. aureus USA300) and gram-negative (Escherichia coli K12) bacteria at different concentrations (0–100 mg/L). The N-CQDs exerted a moderate growth-inhibitory effect on gram-positive bacteria in the dark. However, in the presence of white light generated using 20-W light-emitting diode, N-CQDs could completely kill the bacteria at a low concentration of 20 mg/L. The minimal inhibitory concentration of N-CQDs for the two S. aureus strains (Newman and USA300) was 20 mg/L under light conditions, whereas that for B. subtilis was 100 mg/L. The results demonstrate that N-CQDs may be used as a photosensitizer in aPDT and as an antibacterial agent.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotic resistance has become a serious global public health concern because of the overuse of antibiotics, which leads to environmental pollution and health hazards [1–3]. More seriously, it leads to the emergence and spread of drug-resistant bacteria, making the treatment of infectious diseases challenging. To combat this progressive issue, scientists have demonstrated the feasibility of nanomaterials as an effective alternative to antibiotics. Nanomaterials, such as carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs), have antibacterial properties, and can effectively inhibit bacterial growth and prevent drug resistance [4–7]. CQDs are spherical nanomaterials of size less than 10 nm. These CQDs exhibit good photoluminescence, high water solubility, low toxicity, and good biocompatibility [8,9]. Owing to these attractive features, CQDs have been gaining attention in the past few years.

Several studies have revealed the antibacterial effects of carbon nanomaterials. Phosphorus-doped CQDs (P-doped CQDs), with a diameter of approximately 3.4 nm, have shown considerable antibacterial activity against the gram-negative bacterium Escherichia coli (minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC): 1.23 mg/mL) and gram-positive bacterium Staphylococcus aureus (MIC: 1.44 mg/mL) [10]. Nitrogen-doped CQDs (N-doped CQDs) exhibit antibacterial activity against gram-positive bacteria such as S. aureus (ATCC6538, ATCC43300), Staphylococcus epidermidis, and methicillin-resistant S. aureus (MRSA), with MICs of 0.128–0.256 mg/mL [11]. GQDs, a type of CQDs, are small, glowing nanomaterials with zero dimensions; they are made up of single or multiple layers of graphene sheets [12,13]. Nanomaterials can be used either alone or in combination with a photosensitizer to generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) under light sources of specific wavelengths, inducing DNA damage and achieving antibacterial effects [14–16]. For example, Dwitya et al. created four kinds of N-doped GQDs, which showed antibacterial activity against Bacillus subtilis 3610, with an MIC of 200 mg/L [17]. Kuo et al. synthesized amino-N-GQD nanomaterials with an average size of 8.3 nm, which served as a photosensitizer, and the ROS generated under light of wavelength 670 nm could completely kill MRSA cells [18]. Romero et al. found that carbon dots based on citric acid with an average diameter of 3.81 nm had an excellent antibacterial effect against S. aureus on exposure to blue light (450 nm) [19]. Kim et al. investigated the effect of ZnO and TiO2 nanoparticles (NPs) on E. coli and B. subtilis when pre-illuminated with visible light, UVA (315–400 nm), or UVB (280–315 nm). They found that ZnO NPs were more toxic under UVA or UVB than under visible light irradiation, whereas TiO2 NPs exhibited no such toxicity [20]. These results demonstrate that the antibacterial activity of NPs could be affected by photoreactivity under different pre-illumination conditions.

Antimicrobial photodynamic therapy (aPDT) is an innovative procedure based on the use light of specific wavelengths (380–750 nm) and photosensitizing compounds to generate ROS for bacterial eradication [21–23]. This multi-target approach could reduce the occurrence of drug-resistance in bacteria. In this regard, when excited by light, carbon dots and nano-complexes can induce ROS-mediated DNA damage, thereby promoting antimicrobial effects [24]. Both aPDT and photodynamic therapy (PDT) operate in the visible to near-infrared light spectrum (380–780 nm) with photosensitizers. The key difference between these procedures lies in the scope of their applications, with aPDT being primarily applied for antimicrobial purposes. PDT has a broader range of applications, including cancer treatment [25,26]. Nevertheless, both approaches show a common effect of promoting irreversible chemical reactions that affect biomolecules and the associated components [27].

Carbon dots (CDs) generate ROS, which when produced in excess can damage vital intracellular biomolecules and subsequently lead to cell death. To be effective, light-activated antimicrobial agents need to be in close proximity to the bacteria and exhibit strong absorption within the visible light spectrum for the induction of ROS production [28–30]. Investigation of N-CQDs and their efficacy against the selected bacterial strains under visible light irradiation has not been carried out. Here, we aimed to synthesize N-CQDs and investigate their antibacterial activity against four different bacterial strains, under visible light irradiation.

N-CQDs were synthesized using a previously established method. First, 2 g of anthracene was added to 240 mL of 16 M nitric acid and heated under constant stirring at 80°C for 48 h at 200 rpm. Thereafter, 1 L of deionized water was added to the above solution and stirred at 150 rpm for 1 h. The resulting solution was filtered and dried to obtain 2,4,6-trinitro-phenol (TNP). Subsequently, 2 g of TNP was added to 70 mL of dimethylformamide (DMF) and sonicated for 1 h. The mixture was then placed in a stainless-steel autoclave and reacted under solvothermal conditions at 18°C for 12 h. Finally, the synthesized N-CQDs were stored in dark at room temperature (∼22°C) until further characterization and application.

The N-CQDs were prepared by mixing with 95 % ethanol at 200 mg/L (original solution concentration 70000 mg/L). A 10-μL aliquot of this sample was dropped onto a 400 mesh carbon-coated copper grid (Prod No. 01754-F; TED PELLA, INC., USA) and oven-dried at 50°C for 1–2 days to ensure complete drying without interference. The N-CQDs were characterized using transmission electron microscopy (JEM-3010; JEOL, Tokyo, Japan) at 76 μA and 200 kV.

One gram of freeze-dried N-CQD powder was dissolved in 95 % ethanol, and the solution was dropped onto a slide for Raman spectroscopic measurement (Raman, Nanofinder 30; Tokyo Instruments, INC., Tokyo, Japan). The peak of the Raman spectrum was used to confirm the characteristics of the N-CQDs such as layer number and defects. The spectra were recorded over the range of 120–3200 cm−1.

The freeze-dried N-CQD powder was subjected to pressing and analyzed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (Thermo K-Alpha; Thermo Fisher, Waltham, MA, USA). The chemical composition and content of the N-CQDs were analyzed according to the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectrum. X-Ray diffraction was obtained using an Al k-α radiation source at an accelerating potential of 50 kV.

N-CQDs were prepared with deionized water and DMF at 110 mg/L. A fluorescence decay photon spectrometer (FS5; Edinburgh Instrument, Livingston, UK) was used to measure and record the emission spectra at excitation wavelengths of 300–450 nm.

Five milliliters of B. subtilis NCIB 3610 and E. coli K12 suspensions (at a concentration of 5 × 106 CFU/mL) was treated with 20 mg/L of N-CQDs, incubated at 37°C for 1 h at 200 rpm under 20 W LED white light (HG-LA20; Changhua, Taiwan) or in the dark. The cells were then centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 30 min at 4°C in a high-speed refrigerated centrifuge (CR22G; HITACHI, Tokyo, Japan). The pellet was dissolved in 10 mL PBS, frozen at −80°C overnight, and then freeze-dried. One gram of the freeze-dried sample was used to conduct scanning electron microscopy (SEM, S-3000 N; HITACHI, Tokyo, Japan) at 25 kV, with a magnification range of ×25 to ×20,000.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Growth conditions for various bacterial strains
ce:section.ce:para: Four bacterial strains, B. subtilis 3610, S. aureus Newman, S. aureus USA300, and E. coli K12, were grown on Luria-Bertani (LB) or Mueller-Hinton broth (MHB) agar plates and incubated at 37°C for 18–24 h. The colonies obtained were then inoculated into glass tubes containing 5 mL of LB broth and used for testing the antimicrobial activity of N-CQDs for 14 h.

The bacterial colonies were inoculated in glass tubes containing 5 mL of LB broth and incubated for 12–14 h. The culture was then diluted to 5 × 106 CFU/mL in a conical flask containing 100 mL MHB, and N-CQD solution was added at various concentrations (0, 1, 10, 20, 50, and 100 mg/L). The samples were mixed thoroughly and incubated at 37°C and 200 rpm under 20 W LED white light or in the dark for 3 h. Subsequently, 10 μL of each sample was inoculated on LB agar plates in quadruplicate and incubated at 37°C for 24 h before the colonies were counted.

To assess the ROS level, N-CQDs at concentrations of 0, 10, or 20 mg/L were illuminated in the presence of 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein (DCFH; 1 μM) with white light for different periods (0, 30, 60, 90, 120, 150, 180, 210, and 240 s). DCF fluorescence was measured under the following conditions: excitation wavelength, 480 nm; emission wavelength, 522.6 nm; narrow slot of λex and λem, 2.5 nm; photomultiplier (PMT) voltage, 700 V. The initial fluorescence intensity without white light irradiation was designated as I0. The relative emission intensity (I/I0 – 1) was evaluated at 522.6 nm after different irradiation periods.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Flow cytometry analysis following antibacterial activity testing
ce:section.ce:para: One hundred milliliters of MHB inoculated with S. aureus Newman (1 × 106 CFU/mL) was incubated with 20 mg/L N-CQDs at 37°C for 1 h at 200 rpm under 20 W LED white light or in the dark. Next, 5 mm of the sample was mixed with 10-fold diluted RedoxSensor™ Green reagent (1 mM in DMSO) and 10-fold diluted propidium iodide (20 mM in DMSO) in the dark for 5 min before flow cytometry analysis.

The N-CQDs of concentration 200 mg/L were prepared at pH 4, 7, and 10 using 0.1 N HCl and 0.1 N NaOH, and the surface charge at each pH at 25°C was measured using a zeta potential meter (Zeta Plus, Bic; Brookhaven Instruments Corp., Santa Clara, CA, USA).

The assessment of cytotoxicity of N-CQDs was conducted using the CCK-8 assay (Sigma-Aldrich; St. Louis, MO, USA). 3T3-L1 cells were seeded at 5 × 103 cells/well in 96-well plates. The cells were incubated in the presence of various concentrations of N-CQDs in quadruplicates or the same volume of solvent for each concentration. Cell growth was evaluated using the CCK-8 assay after 48 hours according to the manufacturer’s instructions. The absorbance at 450 nm was measured using a Victor IV microplate reader (PerkinElmer; Waltham, MA, USA).

The synthesized N-CQDs were analyzed using TEM ( Fig. 1a). The particles were spherical with an average diameter of 5.05 nm (Fig. 1c). High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) imaging revealed that the crystals of the N-CQDs had a lattice of 0.119 nm (Fig. 1b). Energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis confirmed that the N-CQDs were mainly composed of carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen (Fig. 1d).

Raman spectroscopic analysis was performed for the structural characterization of N-CQDs ( Fig. 2a). The peaks at 1334.6 and 1574.2 cm−1 corresponded to D and G peaks, respectively. As shown in Fig. 2a, the ID/IG (6305.5 a.u./5057.9 a.u.) intensity ratio was approximately 1.25, indicating that the N-CQDs have a higher degree of defectiveness than their pristine graphene (no defects) counterpart [31].

The UV-Vis absorption spectra of the N-CQDs revealed two absorption peaks at 340 and 529 nm, respectively (Fig. 2b), which can be attributed to the n-π* transition [32]. Fig. 2c shows the emission spectra with excitation wavelengths ranging from 300 to 450 nm and response emission wavelengths close to 350–500 nm. The strongest emission was observed after excitation at 340 nm, with emission peaks appearing at 386, 406, and 429 nm. Upon the same excitation, more than one emission peak was observed, which may be the result of aggregation of the N-CQDs leading to the formation of new compounds [33]. The white LED light wavelength spans from 400 to 700 nm, and N-CQDs are excited between 340 and 529 nm. Importantly, N-CQDs can be efficiently excited by white light, which covers a broad spectrum of wavelengths. In addition, the surface charge of N-CQDs at different pH levels was determined using a zeta potential meter, as shown in Fig. 2d. The zeta potentials at pH 4, 7, and 10 were 34.57, −30.75, and −65.96 mV, respectively.

To evaluate the component ratio of N-CQDs, XPS was conducted. The full scan spectrum of N-CQDs revealed three prominent peaks, with binding energies of 284.97, 405.92, and 532.84 eV, attributed to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively ( Fig. 3a). Fig. 3 shows the surface chemical composition of N-CQDs as analyzed using XPS. The peak values for C-C/CC, C-N, C-OH, O-CO (Fig. 3b), pyrrolic/pyridinic N, NOx (Fig. 3c), C-OH, and CO (Fig. 3d) were 284.5, 285.5, 286.8, 288.0, 399.4, 405.8, 531.9, and 533.3 eV, respectively. These data were used to calculate the ratio of components present in the N-CQDs.

The molar percentages of CC/C-C, C-N, C-OH, and CO were 64.75 %, 24.37 %, 6.72 %, and 4.15 %, respectively ( Table 1). Among the heteroatomic covalent bonds in the N-CQDs, the highest proportion was that of C-N bonds indicating the presence of nitrogen in the N-CQD structure. The proportion of NOx and pyrrolic/pyridinic N was 48.04 % and 51.96 %, respectively, which confirms the successful doping of nitrogen on the CQDs.

Fig. 4 represents the quenching of fluorescence (FL) of N-CQDs and/or energy transfer between DCFH and N-CQDs. The DCFH probe itself is not fluorescent but is oxidized and transformed into fluorescent DCF fluorescein when combined with active oxygen [34]. With an increase in ROS concentration, the DCF fluorescence intensity also increases gradually. The FL spectra for determining ROS levels at different concentrations of N-CQDs are shown in Fig. 4a–c. The FL intensity of DCF at 20 mg/L N-CQDs was higher than that at the other concentrations (0 and 10 mg/L) at all corresponding time points. The calculated relative intensity for peaks in Fig. 4a–c is depicted in Fig. 4d. Our results demonstrated that N-CQDs at concentrations of 10 and 20 mg/L resulted in a rapid increase in ROS production, whereas the control (without N-CQDs) only showed a slow increase.

The antimicrobial activity of N-CQDs was evaluated by adding different concentrations of N-CQDs to cultures of gram-negative (E. coli) and gram-positive (B. subtilis) bacteria, and two different strains of S. aureus in MHB medium and incubating for 3 h under white light or dark conditions. For E. coli, there was no significant difference in the bacterial growth between the control and any of the treated groups ( Fig. 5a). For B. subtilis, compared with that in the control group (0 mg/L), N-CQDs resulted in a 3- to 4-log reduction in cell number at 1, 10, 20, and 50 mg/L under both dark and light conditions. Interestingly, at 100 mg/L, N-CQDs completely halted bacterial growth under the light condition (Fig. 5b). For S. aureus Newman strain, N-CQDs decreased growth by 2- to 7-log at 1, 10, 20, and 50 mg/L under dark conditions; whereas under light conditions, a concentration as low as 20 mg/L was sufficient to completely inhibit bacterial growth (Fig. 5c). Similar results were observed for the S. aureus USA strain, following illumination with white light and treatment with N-CQDs at concentrations of 20 mg/L and above (Fig. 5d).

To examine the mechanism by which N-CQDs act on the bacteria to inhibit cell growth, SEM was used to observe the surface morphology of gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria. The SEM images of B. subtilis and E. coli without treatment with N-CQDs under 5 and 2 μm magnification settings ( Fig. 6a–b and g–h) revealed intact bacteria without any cell rupture. While SEM images of B. subtilis treated with N-CQDs, under light and dark conditions (Fig. 6c–d and e–f), showed rupture of bacterial cell walls, suggesting a bacteriostatic effect. In contrast, the E. coli sample exposed to N-CQDs under light conditions (Fig. 6i–j) displayed only slight depression and shrinkage without any cell rupture, indicating that E. coli is more resistant to N-CQD treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of action of N-CQDs on S. aureus activity
ce:section.ce:para: Research on the use of white light as PDT and aPDT compatible wavelengths remains limited. Beatriz Praena et al. assessed the potential in vitro antiviral activity of Hypericum extract as a photosensitizer combined with white light (420–700 nm, 90 mW/cm2) to combat human coronavirus HCoV-229E [35]. Furthermore, using a chemical precipitation method, Ma et al. synthesized Co-doped β-Bi2O3 that showed enhanced antibacterial effects against E. coli in response to activation under white light (30 W incandescent lamp) illumination [36].

To gain insights into the mechanisms by which N-CQDs eradicate the bacterial strains examined, we stained S. aureus with RedoxSensor™ Green reagent [37–39] and analyzed bacterial reductase activity using flow cytometry. In the control groups, namely, (a) double-distilled H2O (ddH2O; vehicle for N-CQDs), (b) RedoxSensor™ Green reagent only, (c) 20 mg/L N-CQDs, (d) 20 mg/L N-CQDs + RedoxSensor™ Green reagent, and (e) S. aureus only, we merely observed background fluorescence ( Fig. 7a–e). However, when S. aureus was mixed with the RedoxSensor™ Green reagent, 90.2 % of the cells stained positive (Fig. 7f), whereas when S. aureus cells were incubated with 20 mg/L N-CQDs in the dark, only 7.5 % of the cells were stained positive (Fig. 7g). White light exposure contributed to a further reduction in bacterial reductase activity, with only 0.8 % of the cells being stained. These results indicate that N-CQDs contribute to a reduction in bacterial reductase activity, and we speculate that this antibacterial effect is attributable to a disruption of the bacterial cell wall. A comparison between Fig. 7g and h further confirmed the superior antibacterial efficacy of N-CQDs against S. aureus under light conditions.

To assess the toxicity of N-CQDs, the viability of 3T3-L1 cells was analyzed using the CCK-8 assay. When the concentration of N-CQDs in solvent was increased to 100 mg/L, there was no significant difference in relative cell growth values compared with those of the control group ( Fig. 8). This finding suggests the low toxicity of N-CQDs.

Carbon NPs are increasingly used to target bacteria as potential alternatives to current antibacterial agents. In the present study, we synthesized N-CQDs using the hydrothermal method and demonstrated their excellent antimicrobial efficacy against gram-positive bacteria with its light sensitive activity. This is at least in part due to induction of oxidative stress that eventually inhibits bacterial growth.

The incorporation of nitrogen into carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) significantly enhances their structural and functional properties. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis shows that 24.37 % of the groups are C-N, confirming successful nitrogen doping. Additionally, NOx and pyrrolic/pyridinic N are present at 48.04 % and 51.96 %, respectively. These nitrogen species introduce defects into the carbon skeleton, increasing active sites and enhancing catalytic properties [40–42]. The surface chemistry of N-CQDs is also crucial. Positively charged amine groups improve nanomaterial functionalization and biocompatibility, essential for biomedical applications. These groups also enhance the antibacterial properties of N-CQDs, making them suitable for antibacterial coatings and treatments. Overall, nitrogen doping enhances both the internal structure and surface characteristics of CQDs, broadening their application potential.

Gram-positive bacteria such as S. aureus typically exhibit a lower zeta potential (∼ −18 mV at pH 7) compared to gram-negative bacteria such as E. coli (∼ −25 mV at pH 7) [43]. However, N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) display a notably higher negative zeta potential (-34.57 mV at pH 7), aligning closely with the charge of gram-negative bacteria. This difference suggests enhanced electrostatic attraction between N-CQDs and the surface of gram-positive bacteria Zeta potential reflects the electrostatic potential at a particle's surface in solution [44]. The distinct zeta potentials of materials, such as −18 mV for S. aureus and −34.57 mV for N-CQDs, govern their electrostatic interactions. N-CQDs, with their stronger negative charge, are likely to exhibit heightened electrostatic attraction to positively charged surfaces and potentially repel negatively charged particles such as S. aureus. These interactions are pivotal in applications such as nanoparticle binding to bacteria, drug delivery, and biomedical sensing.

Our results also demonstrate that gram-positive bacteria were more sensitive to N-CQDs than gram-negative bacteria, with a better antibacterial effect observed under light than dark conditions. Specifically, the minimum bactericidal concentration (MBC) of B. subtilis was 100 mg/L under light conditions, whereas that of S. aureus Newman and S. aureus USA300 was as low as 20 mg/L. This finding indicates that N-CQDs at 20 mg/L under light conditions may effectively inhibit the growth of other gram-positive bacteria. However, further examination on a higher number of bacteria is required to confirm these results. Among the bacterial strains tested, S. aureus was more sensitive to N-CQDs in terms of the antibacterial effect, which may be attributed to its thinner cell membrane than that of the gram-negative E. coli, allowing better penetration of the cell wall, causing easier disruption [45]. The higher resistance of gram-negative bacteria to N-CQD treatment may be attributed to their double bilayer cell membrane and lack of pores, which make it difficult for nanoparticles to penetrate the cells and exhibit antibacterial activity [46].

CDs with an average diameter of approximately 3.81 nm synthesized using a citric acid-based microwave method (450 nm) reportedly showed strong antibacterial effects against S. aureus when activated using blue light [19]. When applied at concentrations of 6.9 and 13.8 mg/mL in conjunction with energy density of 20 and 40 J/cm2, these CDs contributed to significant reductions in bacterial counts of approximately 5 and 4 log CFU/mL, respectively. These antimicrobial effects involve the initial adherence of the CDs to bacterial surfaces, with a subsequent generation of ROS in response to light exposure, thereby disrupting cell walls and membranes, causing DNA/RNA damage, modifying gene expression, and ultimately leading to cell death. Kuo et al. synthesized amino-N-GQDs (average size ∼8.3 nm) as photosensitizers, which when excited at 670 nm for 60 s followed by 3 h of incubation, effectively eliminated MRSA cells [18]. Furthermore, Kim et al. assessed the antibacterial activities of ZnO and TiO2 NPs under pre-irradiation conditions [20]. ZnO NPs pre-irradiated with UVA and UVB were demonstrated to have substantial antibacterial effects, reducing the colony counts of E. coli and spore counts of B. subtilis. The observed variable toxicity of ZnO NPs under different pre-irradiation conditions may be attributed to the induction of oxidative stress and inhibition of bacterial growth mediated via Zn2+ ion photo-dissolution. In our study, we employed white light LEDs with wavelengths ranging between 365 and 450 nm to stimulate and potentially eradicate bacteria. Compared with photo-stimulation using UV light, which is harmful to humans, our approach is considered a safe and ecofriendly alternative. Additionally, the findings of this study highlight the pronounced bactericidal efficacy of our approach, which can contribute to 100 % eradication of both Bacillus and Staphylococcus species.

We successfully synthesized N-CQDs with an average diameter of 5.05 nm using a hydrothermal method and demonstrated their antibacterial properties. These N-CQDs act as photosensitizers, generating ROS under white light. They adhere to the bacterial membranes and disrupt their integrity, leading to bacterial death. Moreover, these N-CQDs exhibited low cytotoxicity when tested on 3T3-L1 cells, making them a promising candidate for antibacterial treatment. Overall, these findings lay a foundation for developing alternative antimicrobial treatments to effectively combat drug resistance in bacteria.

Yi-Huang Hsueh: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Methodology, Conceptualization. Ya-Yun Zheng: Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Jen-Shyang Ni: Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Sin-Jen Lee: Methodology, Data curation. Kuang-Tzu Huang: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There is no competing interest to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was funded by the National Science and Technology Council of Taiwan to Yi-Huang Hsueh, grant numbers 112-2221-E992-012 and 113-2221-E-992 -009 -MY2 and by Chang Gung Medical Foundation of Taiwan to Kuang-Tzu Huang, grant number CMRPG8N0441.
